| First game            = {{fb|GHA}} 6–1 {{fb-rt|GUI}}<br />(Ghana; 6 December 1958)
| Largest win           = {{fb|GUI}} 14–0 {{fb-rt|MTN|1959}}<br />(Guinea; 20 May 1972)
| Largest loss          = {{fb|ZAI}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|GUI}}<br />([[Zaire]]; 2 July 1972)
| Regional name         = [[Africa Cup of Nations]]
| Regional cup apps     = 14
| Regional cup first    = [[1970 African Cup of Nations|1970]]
| Regional cup best     = Runners-up ([[1976 African Cup of Nations|1976]])
| 2ndRegional name      = [[African Nations Championship]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 3
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[2016 African Nations Championship|2016]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  =  Third place ([[2020 African Nations Championship|2020]])
| 3rdRegional name      = [[Amílcar Cabral Cup]]
| 3rdRegional cup apps  = 18
| 3rdRegional cup first = [[1979 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1979]]
| 3rdRegional cup best  = '''Champions''' ([[1981 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1981]], [[1982 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1982]], [[1987 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1987]], [[1988 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1988]], [[2005 Amílcar Cabral Cup|2005]])
}}
The '''Guinea national football team''' ([[French language|French]]: '' Équipe de football de Guinée'') represents [[Guinea]] in men's international [[association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Guinean Football Federation]]. They have never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] finals, and their best finish in the [[Africa Cup of Nations]] was runners-up in [[1976 African Nations Cup|1976]]. The team reached the quarter-finals in four recent tournaments ([[2004 Africa Cup of Nations|2004]], [[2006 Africa Cup of Nations|2006]], [[2008 Africa Cup of Nations|2008]] and [[2015 Africa Cup of Nations|2015]]). The team represents both [[FIFA]] and [[Confederation of African Football]] (CAF).

==History==
Guinea made their footballing debut in an away [[Exhibition game#Association football|friendly]] on 6 December 1958, losing 6–1 against [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesg/guinea-intres.html |title=Guinea – List of International Matches |author=Barrie Courtney |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=16 December 2024 |archive-date=2 December 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241202132308/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesg/guinea-intres.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In 1963, Guinea entered its first qualification campaign for an [[Africa Cup of Nations]], the [[1963 Africa Cup of Nations|1963 tournament]] in [[Ghana]]. Drawn in a two-legged qualifier against [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]], Guinea drew the first leg 2–2 away on 27 July, and on 6 October won 1–0 at home to win 3–2 on aggregate. They were later disqualified for using Guinean officials in the second leg, and Nigeria went through to the finals in their place. In 1965, Guinea entered the [[1965 African Cup of Nations qualification|qualifiers]] for the [[1965 African Cup of Nations]] in [[Tunisia]] and was placed in Group A with [[Senegal national football team|Senegal]] and [[Mali national football team|Mali]]. On 28 February, they lost 2–0 in Senegal before beating them 3–0 at home on 31 March, Senegal's win over Mali allowed them to qualify instead of Guinea.<ref name="rsssf-internationals">{{cite web | url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesg/guinea-intres.html | title=Guinea – List of International Matches | publisher=Rec.Sport.Soccer Statistics Foundation | access-date=1 February 2012 | archive-date=7 August 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220807185532/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesg/guinea-intres.html | url-status=live }}</ref>

During the [[1976 African Cup of Nations]] the Guinean team finished second to [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]], only missing out on the championship by a point.<ref name="fifa1">{{cite web | url=https://www.fifa.com/associations/association=gui/countryInfo.html | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070630204002/http://www.fifa.com/associations/association=gui/countryInfo.html | url-status=dead | archive-date=June 30, 2007 | title=Guinea: Country Info | publisher=FIFA | access-date=1 February 2012}}</ref><ref name="rsssf-1976">{{cite web | url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/76a.html | title=African Nations Cup 1976 | publisher=Rec.Sport.Soccer Statistics Foundation | access-date=1 February 2012 | archive-date=22 August 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220822095118/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/76a.html | url-status=live }}</ref>

In 2001, FIFA expelled the country from the qualification process of the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002 World Cup]] and [[2002 African Cup of Nations]] due to government interference in football.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/1229865.stm|title=Fifa confirm Guinea ban|work=BBC Sport|date=19 March 2001|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=6 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306005609/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/1229865.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> They returned to international action in September 2002 after a two-year ban from competition.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/cup_of_nations/2238897.stm|title=Guinea make their return|work=BBC Sport|date=5 September 2002|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=20 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150120143913/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/cup_of_nations/2238897.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> In the [[2004 African Cup of Nations]], Guinea reached the quarter-finals, scoring the first goal against [[Mali national football team|Mali]] before ultimately losing 2–1, conceding the winning goal in the last minute of the match.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/3465607.stm|title=Mali squeeze through|work=BBC Sport|date=7 February 2004|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=20 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150120143719/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/3465607.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> Guinea reached the quarter-final stage again in the [[2006 Africa Cup of Nations|2006 tournament]], taking the lead against [[Senegal national football team|Senegal]] before losing 3–2.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/4672266.stm|title=Guinea 2–3 Senegal|work=BBC Sport|date=3 February 2006|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=20 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150120135259/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/africa/4672266.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> 2008 saw Guinea reach the quarter-finals of the Africa Cup of Nations for a third successive tournament, only to suffer a 5–0 defeat against [[Ivory Coast national football team|Côte d'Ivoire]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/africa/7224152.stm|title=Quarter-finals: Civ 5–0 Gui|work=BBC Sport|date=4 February 2008|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=9 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170909143721/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/africa/7224152.stm|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2012, Guinea beat [[Botswana national football team|Botswana]] 6–1 in the group stage of the [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations]], becoming the first side to score six goals in an [[Africa Cup of Nations]] game since [[Ivory Coast national football team|Côte d'Ivoire]] in 1970.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/16767266|title=Nations Cup: Guinea crush Botswana|work=BBC Sport|date=28 January 2012|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=18 February 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120218132315/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/16767266|url-status=live}}</ref> The team subsequently exited the tournament at the group stage after a draw against Ghana.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/16824223|title=Nations Cup: Ghana through after 1–1 draw with Guinea|work=BBC Sport|date=1 February 2012|access-date=18 January 2013|archive-date=5 February 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120205065053/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/16824223|url-status=live}}</ref>

On 4 January 2016, [[Confederation of African Football|CAF]] lifted a ban on Guinea playing their home international in [[Guinea]] after it was declared free of [[Ebola virus epidemic in Guinea|Ebola]] by the U.N. World Health Organization in December 2015.<ref>{{cite web|title=Soccer-Guinea cleared to host matches after being declared Ebola-free|url=http://uk.reuters.com/article/soccer-africa-guinea-ebola-idUKL3N14O3DM20160104|website=uk.reuters.com/|publisher=[[Reuters]]|access-date=4 January 2016|date=4 January 2016|archive-date=4 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304125508/http://uk.reuters.com/article/soccer-africa-guinea-ebola-idUKL3N14O3DM20160104|url-status=dead}}</ref>

===Coaching history===
[[File:0K8A0250 (54855751638) (cropped).jpg|thumb|150px|[[Paulo Duarte (footballer)|Paulo Duarte]] has been the manager of Guinea since 2025]]
{{Div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|ROU|1965}} [[Petre Moldoveanu]] (1975–77)<ref>Petre Moldoveanu who won the [[CAF Champions League]] in 1975 with [[Hafia Football Club]] was appointed manager of Guinea and led his side to the [[1976 African Cup of Nations]] finals.</ref>
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Serge Devèze]] (1992–93)
* {{flagicon|BIH|1992}} [[Boro Primorac]] (1994)
* {{flagicon|UKR|1992}} [[Mykhaylo Fomenko]] (1994)
* {{flagicon|UKR|1992}} [[Volodymyr Muntyan]] (1995–98)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Henri Stambouli]] (1998–99)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Bruno Metsu]] (2000)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Bernard Simondi]] (2000–01)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Michel Dussuyer]] (2002–04)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Patrice Neveu]] (2004–06)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Robert Nouzaret]] (2006–09)
* {{flagicon|GUI}} [[Titi Camara]] (2009)
* {{flagicon|GUI}} [[Mamadi Souaré]] (2009–10)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Michel Dussuyer]] (2010–15)
* {{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Luis Fernández]] (2015–16)
* {{flagicon|GUI}} [[Lappé Bangoura]] (2016–18)
* {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Paul Put]] (2018–19)
* {{flagicon|FRA}} [[Didier Six]] (2019–21)
* {{flagicon|GUI}} [[Kaba Diawara]] (2021–24)
* {{flagicon|GUI}} [[Souleymane Camara]] (2024–25)
* {{flagicon|FRA}} [[Charles Paquille]] (2024)
* {{flagicon|FRA}} [[Michel Dussuyer]] (2024–25)
* {{flagicon|POR}} [[Paulo Duarte (footballer)|Paulo Duarte]] (2025–)
{{div col end}}

==Players==

===Current squad===
The following players were called up for the [[Exhibition game#Association football|friendly matches]] against [[Northern Ireland national football team|Northern Ireland]] on 4 June 2026.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.feguifoot.com/2026/05/24/liste-des-joueurs-convoques-pour-le-match-amical-face-a-lirlande-du-nord/|title=Liste des joueurs convoqués pour le match amical face à l’Irlande du Nord|publisher=[[Guinean Football Federation]]|date=24 May 2026|language=fr}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.feguifoot.com/2026/06/02/point-sur-le-regroupement-du-syli-national-avant-le-match-amical-face-a-lirlande-du-nord/|title=Point sur le regroupement du Syli National avant le match amical face à l’Irlande du Nord|publisher=Guinean Football Federation|date=2 June 2026|language=fr}}</ref>

''Caps and goals are correct as of 31 March 2026, after the match against [[Benin national football team|Benin]].''

{{nat fs g start}}

{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>DEC</sup> = Player refused to join the team after the call-up.
*<sup>INJ</sup> = Player withdrew from the squad due to an injury.
*<sup>PRE</sup> = Preliminary squad.
*<sup>RET</sup> = Player has retired from international football.
*<sup>SUS</sup> = Suspended from the national team.
*<sup>WD</sup> = Player withdrew from the squad for non-injury related reasons.
{{nat fs end}}

==Records==
{{Updated|8 September 2025}}<ref>{{cite web |last1=Mamrud |first1=Roberto |title=Appearances for Guinea National Team |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/guinea-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2023-02-03 |archive-date=2022-07-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220710163828/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/guinea-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Guinea.''

===Most appearances===
[[File:Issiaga Sylla1.jpg|175px|right|thumb|[[Issiaga Sylla]] is Guinea's most capped player with 90 appearances.]]

|-

|-
| 6 ||align=left| [[Chérif Souleymane]] || 68 || 20 || 1964–1977
|-

| 8 ||align=left| [[Naby Yattara]] || 61 || 0 || 2007–2019

| 9 ||align=left| [[Fodé Mansaré]] || 60 || 8 || 2002–2010
|-

|-
|}

==Competitive record==

==Honours==
[[File:24A2DL (53494653775).jpg|thumb|Guinea squad at the [[2023 Africa Cup of Nations]].]]
{{col-begin|width=60%}}
{{Col-3}}

===Continental===
* '''[[Africa Cup of Nations|CAF African Cup of Nations]]'''
** [[File:Silver medal africa.svg|15px]] Runners-up (1): [[1976 Africa Cup of Nations|1976]]
* '''[[Football at the African Games|African Games]]'''<sup>'''1'''</sup>
** [[File:Silver medal africa.svg|15px]] Silver medal (1): [[Football at the 1973 All-Africa Games|1973]]
{{Col-2}}

===Regional===
* '''[[Amílcar Cabral Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (5)''': [[1981 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1981]], [[1982 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1982]], [[1987 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1987]], [[1988 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1988]], [[2005 Amílcar Cabral Cup|2005]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1989 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1989]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (4): [[1979 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1979]], [[1980 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1980]], [[1997 Amílcar Cabral Cup|1997]], [[2000 Amílcar Cabral Cup|2000]]
* '''[[CEDEAO Cup]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[CEDEAO Cup#1985 CEDEAO Cup|1985]]

===Friendly===
* '''[[FIFA Series]] (1):''' [[2024 FIFA Series#Saudi Arabia B|2024 Saudi Arabia B]]
{{Col-end}}

===Summary===
{| class="wikitable" width=30% style="font-size:90%; text-align:center;"
|-

|-
| align=left|[[African Cup of Nations|CAF African Cup of Nations]]
|0 || 1 || 0 || 1

|}
;Notes
# Competition organized by [[Association of National Olympic Committees of Africa|ANOCA]], officially not recognized by FIFA.
